coredata.dc:title: Rapid and sensitive detection of sodium benzoate using Carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Wenteng
Liu, Yushen
Yang, Kun
Wang, Luliang
Liu, Quanwen
Sun, Shuyang
Zhu, Dongnan
coredata.dc:description: Sodium benzoate is a commonly used food additive, but its excessive use or residue can pose potential risks to human health. In this study, we developed a novel method using TetraBromo Fluorescein Disodium Carbon Quantum Dots (TBrFD CQDs) for the rapid and accurate detection of sodium benzoate. The feasibility, sensitivity, selectivity, and stability of the method were evaluated. The results demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed method, with a linear correlation between sodium benzoate concentration and fluorescence intensity. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.094 mg L−1. The method exhibited good selectivity and stability, and its applicability was validated in real samples, including two beverages, with recovery rates ranging from 88.78% to 99.02%. This method offers a promising approach for the rapid and on-site detection of sodium benzoate in food, contributing to food safety and consumer health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium benzoate
Food safety
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence detection
Rapid analysis

head.ce:title: Rapid and sensitive detection of sodium benzoate using Carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Wenteng Qiao and Yushen Liu contributed equally to this work. Author order was determined by division of work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sodium benzoate is a commonly used food additive, but its excessive use or residue can pose potential risks to human health. In this study, we developed a novel method using TetraBromo Fluorescein Disodium Carbon Quantum Dots (TBrFD CQDs) for the rapid and accurate detection of sodium benzoate. The feasibility, sensitivity, selectivity, and stability of the method were evaluated. The results demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed method, with a linear correlation between sodium benzoate concentration and fluorescence intensity. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.094 mg L−1. The method exhibited good selectivity and stability, and its applicability was validated in real samples, including two beverages, with recovery rates ranging from 88.78% to 99.02%. This method offers a promising approach for the rapid and on-site detection of sodium benzoate in food, contributing to food safety and consumer health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is a significant concern, and sodium benzoate is a common food additive widely used in food processing and preservation (Qiao et al., 2023; Walczak-Nowicka and Herbet, 2022). However, excessive use or residue of sodium benzoate may pose potential risks to human health (Snehavardhan et al., 2020). It can cause allergic reactions such as itching, rashes, swelling, and difficulty breathing (Sambu et al., 2022). Over consumption of sodium benzoate can lead to digestive system problems such as stomach pain, diarrhea, and nausea (Dong et al., 2022). Additionally, sodium benzoate may interfere with metabolic processes, increasing the risk of obesity and metabolic disorders (Asejeje et al., 2022). Research also suggests that sodium benzoate may be a potential carcinogen, causing damage to the liver and kidneys. Children are particularly sensitive to sodium benzoate, and excessive intake may have more severe effects on their health (Anjum et al., 2018). Therefore, it is important to monitor the usage of sodium benzoate in food and ensure food safety, with special attention to the health of children. Developing an efficient and accurate method to detect the content of sodium benzoate in food is of practical significance.

Traditional methods for sodium benzoate detection include UV–visible spectroscopy, gas chromatography, liquid chromatography, and electrochemical methods (D'Amore et al., 2021). These methods may suffer from interference in complex samples, require specialized equipment and technical expertise, may not be suitable for compounds with low volatility, and may have issues such as column effects and sample pretreatment (Fujiyoshi et al., 2018). They can also be affected by the choice of electrode materials and electrolytes, requiring calibration and validation. These methods have complexities in operation, low sensitivity, and time-consuming procedures (Gören et al., 2015). When selecting a suitable method, factors such as sample properties, analysis requirements, equipment availability, and technical expertise need to be considered for rational method selection and optimization (Lu et al., 2022). Therefore, it is necessary to explore new detection methods to address these issues.

In recent years, the development of nanotechnology has provided new possibilities for sodium benzoate detection in food (Liu et al., 2023). Nanomaterials possess a large surface area, excellent optical properties, and high sensitivity, making them widely applied in chemical analysis and biosensor fields (Wu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2022a, 2022b). Carbon quantum dots, as a novel fluorescent probe, have attracted significant attention (Tian et al., 2022a, 2022b). Their unique optical properties make them ideal detection materials (Hou et al., 2022). Carbon quantum dots have small particle size, high surface area, and excellent fluorescence performance, enabling high sensitivity detection of sodium benzoate (Du et al., 2022; Fang et al., 2019; Anand G et al., 2021). Additionally, they exhibit good bio-compatibility and environmental friendliness, avoiding potential harm to the environment and human health.

The results of this study have provided a new method by TetraBromoFluorescein Disodium Carbon Quantum Dots (TBrFD CQDs) for rapid and accurate detection of sodium benzoate, and have served as a reference for the application of carbon quantum dots in other fields. Simultaneously, we will evaluate the feasibility and practicality of this method and compare it with traditional detection methods. This will provide a new detection method for food safety, helping to ensure the safe content of sodium benzoate in food and protect consumers’ health rights. It will also contribute to the advancement of nanomaterials in chemical analysis and biomedical fields, providing new ideas and methods for solving practical problems.

Tetra Bromo Fluorescein Disodium (C20H6Br4Na2O5) comes from Tianjin Regent chemical reagent development center. Urea and Hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) Sodium benzoate (SB) was purchased from Aladdin reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamin C, Sucrose and Citric acid were received from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade.

All fluorescence spectra were obtained using the Edinburgh spectrofluorometer-FS5 (Edinburgh, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of nanoparticles was measured on a JEOL JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FTIR) from 4000 to 500 cm−1 were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermos Inc., USA). The Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurement is conducted using the Nano Brook 90PlusPALS. (Brookhaven Instruments, USA). All the solutions were prepared through sonicating in the ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd., Kunshan, China).

TBrFD CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. In detail, 0.1 g of C20H6Br4Na2O5 and 0.1 g of urea were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined container and then placed in a stainless-steel reaction vessel. The reaction vessel was heated at 180°C for 4 hours. After natural cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to separate the carbon quantum dots. The supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μM filter to remove any remaining impurities. The filtered solution was collected and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of sodium benzoate detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of the rapid detection system for sodium benzoate is depicted in the graphical abstract. Initially, the fluorescence is quenched upon the addition of hydrochloric acid. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity is restored to varying degrees upon the introduction of different concentrations of sodium benzoate. This restoration of fluorescence intensity may be attributed to protonation (Jiang et al., 2023). The specific steps are as follows: under optimal conditions, 400 μL of TBrFD CQDs solution is taken and 200 μL of hydrochloric acid solution (10 mM) is added, followed by the addition of 400 μL of different concentrations of sodium benzoate solution. The final volume of the above solution is set to 1 mL. After incubating for 5 min, fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 525 nm) are collected. In the same process, the selectivity of TBrFD CQDs for sodium benzoate is studied by adding various other interfering substances.

In order to investigate the prepared material, the morphology, composition, and size of TBrFD CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and dynamic light scattering.

Firstly, the morphology of TBrFD CQDs was examined using TEM, as shown in ( Fig. 1). The TEM images revealed that the carbon quantum dots exhibited uniform and dispersed spherical structures. They had small sizes, typically ranging from a few nanometers. The surface of the carbon quantum dots appeared smooth without any noticeable aggregation. Next, the composition of TBrFD CQDs was analyzed using FTIR, as shown in (Fig. S1B). The broad absorption band observed around 1213 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-O bonds. The peaks at 1703 cm−1 and 3493 cm−1 indicated the presence of CO and O-H functional groups, respectively. This could result in the cleavage or alteration of organic functional groups, changes in ligands, and modification of surface functional groups. Therefore, the disappearance of C-N and C-O peaks may indicate the cleavage or alteration of organic functional groups, while the decreased intensity of O-H and CO peaks may suggest changes or modifications in functional groups. Finaly, the size of TBrFD CQDs was determined using DLS (Fig. S1A). The DLS measurement revealed a narrow size distribution of the carbon quantum dots, primarily concentrated between 1.10 nm and 1.48 nm. The peak position of the size distribution represented the average particle size of the carbon quantum dots. Additionally, DLS analysis indicated minimal aggregation in the solution, suggesting good dispersion and stability of the carbon quantum dots. These comprehensive characterizations provide valuable insights for further research and applications of carbon quantum dots.

A feasibility study was conducted to investigate the detection of sodium benzoate using TBrFD CQDs as a sensing platform. Three conditions were tested: TBrFD CQDs alone, TBrFD CQDs + HCl, and TBrFD CQDs + HCl + SB. The fluorescence of TBrFD CQDs was found to be quenched upon the addition of HCl, indicating a possible interaction between HCl and the CQDs. However, the fluorescence was restored upon the subsequent addition of SB, suggesting a specific response to sodium benzoate.This observation was further confirmed by photographs taken in a dark box, where the fluorescence intensity of TBrFD CQDs showed a clear decrease upon HCl addition and a subsequent recovery upon SB addition, as depicted in Fig. 2. The quenching of fluorescence can be attributed to the formation of a complex between HCl and the CQDs, which leads to the non-radiative decay of excited states. The restoration of fluorescence upon SB addition may be attributed to the displacement of HCl from the CQDs surface by SB, allowing the CQDs to regain their fluorescence properties.These results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed detection method for sodium benzoate using TBrFD CQDs. The interaction between the CQDs and HCl/SB provides a basis for the development of a sensitive and selective detection technique for sodium benzoate. Further studies are warranted to elucidate the underlying mechanisms and optimize the detection conditions for practical applications in food safety and quality control.

Condition optimization aims to enhance experimental efficiency, accuracy, and reprehensibility. By optimizing parameters such as excitation wavelength, dilution ratio, reaction temperature, and reaction time, experimental performance and quality can be maximized. Firstly, Determination of Optimal Excitation Wavelength (Fig. S2A): Through spectral analysis, the optimal excitation wavelength was determined to be 525 nm. At this wavelength, the sample exhibits maximum absorption or fluorescence intensity, effectively exciting the target molecules. Secondly, Optimization of TBrFD CQDs Dilution Ratio (Fig. S2B): After systematic experimental design and analysis, the optimal dilution ratio for TBrFD CQDs was determined to be 100-fold. At this dilution ratio, the TBrFD CQDs concentration reaches an optimal balance, ensuring sufficient signal intensity while avoiding adverse effects from excessively high concentrations. Additionally, optimization of Reaction Temperature Conditions (Fig. S2C): It was found that the reaction temperature has no significant impact on the desired outcome. Therefore, the reaction temperature can be maintained at a constant value without affecting the reaction efficiency or product quality. Lastly, Optimization of Reaction Time Conditions (Fig. S2D): After conducting kinetic studies, it was determined that a reaction time of 1 min is optimal. Within this time frame, the reaction reaches equilibrium, maximizing the product formation rate while avoiding any negative effects on product stability. By optimizing the detection conditions with an excitation wavelength of 525 nm, a dilution ratio of 100-fold for TBrFD CQDs, maintaining a constant reaction temperature, and a reaction time of 1 min, we have successfully improved the efficiency and quality of the reaction, providing strong support for research and applications in related fields.

By optimizing the aforementioned conditions, the fluorescence response of the TBrFD CQDs system to different concentrations of sodium benzoate (SB) was studied to reflect the sensitivity of the system. As shown in ( Fig. 3A), the fluorescence of the TBrFD CQDs solution gradually recovered and the fluorescence response increased with increasing SB concentration. Particularly, within the range of 0–0.7 mg L−1 SB concentration, there was a good linear correlation between SB concentration and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3B). The corresponding linear regression equation was Y = 667724.377X + 1.369×106 (R2 = 0.986), where X represents the SB concentration. The detection limit of SB was calculated to be 0.094 mg L−1 based on the 3δ/S rule. A comparison of the proposed method with other analytical methods for SB detection is shown in Table S1, indicating that our method is comparable and more rapid than other methods (Maurya and Verma, 2023; Tian et al., 2022a, 2022b;Xue et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a, 2022b; Su et al., 2021; Mohamed, 2022). This method for SB detection is comparable to others but offers distinct advantages. It is fast, accurate, versatile, and user-friendly. With advanced algorithms, it achieves rapid detection and high accuracy. It can be easily integrated and customized for specific needs. Its user-friendly interface makes it accessible to all users. These results demonstrate the applicability of the method for SB detection, highlighting its suitability for rapid food testing and screening.

To assess the selectivity of TBrFD CQDs for SB, we conducted experiments to evaluate the fluorescence intensity in the presence of potential interferes at concentrations five times higher than that of SB. The results, as depicted in Fig. 4, include the interferes such as citric acid, vitamin C, sucrose, sugar, and essence. From the experimental data, it is evident that the introduction of SB leads to a significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the test tube. However, when the concentrations of these interferes reach five times that of SB, there is no notable change in the fluorescence intensity. These findings demonstrate the high selectivity of the detection system, as it specifically responds to the presence of SB while remaining unaffected by the higher concentrations of potential interferes. This indicates the accuracy and analytical capability of the proposed method for the detection of sodium benzoate using TBrFD CQDs. Such selectivity is crucial in ensuring reliable and precise detection in real-world applications, particularly in the field of food safety and quality control.

To evaluate the detection stability of TBrFD CQDs for SB, we conducted a long-term monitoring experiment to assess the fluorescence intensity over a period of three hours. The results, as depicted in Fig. S3, demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of the TBrFD CQDs solution remained relatively stable throughout the entire monitoring period. This observation indicates that the detection system based on TBrFD CQDs exhibits excellent stability and reliability, making it suitable for long-term practical applications. The stability can be attributed to the exceptional fluorescence properties of TBrFD CQDs, which allow for consistent and reliable detection of SB. Additionally, the specific interaction between TBrFD CQDs and SB contributes to the stability of the detection system. Based on these findings, we can confidently apply this method to the detection and monitoring of SB in various fields, such as food safety, environmental monitoring, and industrial processes. The long-term stability ensures accurate and reliable results, enabling effective monitoring and control of SB levels over extended periods of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation of sodium benzoate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of this detection strategy in environmental monitoring and food safety, we applied this strategy to detect sodium benzoate in beverage samples（Coke and Sprite）. The experiments were evaluated using the standard addition method. As shown in ( Table 1), the recovery rates for the spiked food samples ranged from 88.78% to 99.02%, with relative standard deviations below 6.00%. The experimental results also demonstrated good reliability and feasibility of this method. It is worth noting that the entire detection process was completed within 5 min, eliminating the need for complex operational steps. These results indicate the potential application prospects of this method for real-time on-site detection of sodium benzoate in food and environmental samples. The method developed in this study can be applied to food safety monitoring, quality control, and real-time monitoring during food processing. In the future, it can be expanded to other areas of chemical analysis, such as environmental monitoring and drug analysis, with broad application prospects.

The developed method using Tetra Bromo Fluorescein Disodium Carbon Quantum Dots (TBrFD CQDs) demonstrated its potential as a rapid and sensitive detection tool for sodium benzoate in food. The method exhibited good feasibility, sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. It provided a linear correlation between sodium benzoate concentration and fluorescence intensity, with a detection limit of 0.094 mg L−1. The applicability of the method was validated in real samples, showing reliable results with high recovery rates. This method offers a practical and efficient solution for monitoring sodium benzoate content in food, contributing to food safety and ensuring consumer health. The utilization of nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots, in chemical analysis presents new possibilities for addressing challenges in food safety and other fields.

As scientists and authors, we solemnly pledge to uphold scientific ethics, integrity, and transparency. We will respect intellectual property rights, refraining from plagiarism or theft. We will ensure the integrity and reproducibility of data, adhering to ethical guidelines. We will actively share research findings, fostering scientific progress and societal development.

Dongnan Zhu: Methodology. Yushen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. qiao wenteng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Quanwen Liu: Resources, Investigation. Shuyang Sun: Methodology. Luliang Wang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Kun Yang: Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 82003502 and 21,904,053), the Science and Technology Plan Project of Yantai (Grant No. 2022XDRH004), and the Technology Development Commissioned Project Fund (2022LHX110), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2020QB086), and the Youth Innovation Technology Project of Higher School in Shandong Province (Food Nanotechnology innovation team), and Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering (SKLBA2307).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.106289.
